A strong pre-frontal squall line crossed the area late morning through mid afternoon. Strong wind shear and helicity promoted rotation cells as well as bowing line segments. One comma-head storm formed along the Interstate 10 corridor near Live Oak, and this cell raced eastward toward Jacksonville. The storm had a long-lived mesocylone, and the rear flank downdraft descended downward several times causing brief periods of strong low level rotation which caused a few tornado touchdowns. The RFD produced strong winds of 50-80 mph and caused widespread tree damage and some structural damage as the comma-head raced across north Florida, including the Jacksonville metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down in St. Marys, some fell on homes and parked cars. The time of damage was based on radar and timing of wind gusts from area observation stations.

With dry air in place (PWATs below 2 inches), strong wind gusts were a hazard in resultant thunderstorms over a very unstable air mass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mean ridge was building over the Fl peninsula while fast westerly steering flow of 15-20 mph prevailed over SE Georgia. The combination of an unstable airmass, high moisture and forcing from outflow boundaries triggered a few severe storms in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at the intersection of Orange and Lincoln Streets in the town of homeland. The time of damage was based on radar data. The cost of damage was estimated (truly unknown) for the event to be included in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front pushed through the Southeast on the 27th, leaving very cold air in its wake.  Behind the front, an area of moisture pushed north out of the Gulf on the 28th, spreading precipitation across north and central Georgia.  With the surface cold air in place, the precipitation began as a mix of light sleet and snow across west central Georgia during the mid-morning hours on the 28th, and as the cold air continued to push south, rain across central Georgia changed over to freezing rain, ending as a sleet/snow mix by the evening of the 28th.||The complicating factor for this event was the fact that the high temperature for most of north Georgia occurred early on the 28th, with the strong cold air continuing to funnel in from the north behind the arctic front.  Temperatures fell through the day, and at onset of snow in the Atlanta Metro area, temperatures were in the upper 20s.  Massive gridlock ensued as businesses and schools let out just after noon on the 28th, with thousands of motorists stuck on roads for hours if not overnight.  The significant societal impacts from this event made national news because of the gridlock in the Atlanta Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted north and central Georgia on the 28th.  Snow and sleet began late Tuesday morning, accumulating to widespread amounts of 1 to 3 inches of snow.  Across the Metro Atlanta area, thousands of motorists were trapped on icy roads for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front blasted through northeast Georgia during the morning of the 6th, bringing gusty winds and the coldest air mass to have affected the region since 1994. By early evening, winds of 10 to 20 mph, with stronger gusts combined with temperatures falling into the 20s and teens to produce wind chill values below 0 across the Georgia. Wind chills across the higher elevations likely reached -15 or lower briefly on the night of the 6th. Although wind gradually diminished overnight, low temperatures on the 7th ranged from a little below zero above 3000 feet to the single digits across the Piedmont and foothills.

Behind the front, an area of moisture pushed north out of the Gulf on the 28th, spreading precipitation across north and central Georgia.  With the surface cold air in place, the precipitation began as a mix of light sleet and snow across west central Georgia during the mid-morning hours on the 28th, and as the cold air continued to push south, rain across central Georgia changed over to freezing rain, ending as a sleet/snow mix by the evening of the 28th.||The complicating factor for this event was the fact that the high temperature for most of north Georgia occurred early on the 28th, with the strong cold air continuing to funnel in from the north behind the arctic front.  Temperatures fell through the day, and at onset of snow in the Atlanta Metro area, temperatures were in the upper 20s.  Massive gridlock ensued as businesses and schools let out just after noon on the 28th, with thousands of motorists stuck on roads for hours if not overnight.  The significant societal impacts from this event made national news because of the gridlock in the Atlanta Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted north and central Georgia on the 28th.  Snow and sleet began late Tuesday morning, accumulating to widespread amounts of 1 to 3 inches of snow.  Across the Metro Atlanta area, thousands of motorists were trapped on icy roads for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front blasted through northeast Georgia during the morning of the 6th, bringing gusty winds and the coldest air mass to have affected the region since 1994. By early evening, winds of 10 to 20 mph, with stronger gusts combined with temperatures falling into the 20s and teens to produce wind chill values below 0 across the Georgia. Wind chills across the higher elevations likely reached -15 or lower briefly on the night of the 6th. Although wind gradually diminished overnight, low temperatures on the 7th ranged from a little below zero above 3000 feet to the single digits across the Piedmont and foothills.

Very warm and moist air from the Gulf of Mexico prevailed over Georgia in advance of a strong storm system that moved through the eastern United States. During the early morning hours a line of thunderstorms moved through parts of West Central Georgia and continued into the Atlanta metropolitan area and eventually portions of East and Central Georgia. A low-level jet developed on the back side of this line of thunderstorms which was conducive for the development of damaging winds and tornadoes. This line of thunderstorms with heavy rain interacted with a dissipating stationary front early in the morning over North Georgia to produce flash flooding in Dade County in Northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fannin County 911 Center reported dozens of trees down countywide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the area from the west early on the 11th as an area of cold air damming in place across northeast Georgia began retreating into the Carolinas.  Showers and a few thunderstorms pushed across north Georgia during the early morning hours, but then right after sunrise as the cold front approached the wedge front, thunderstorms intensified and became severe with mainly wind damage reported. Heavy downpours of rain caused localized flash flooding in the Atlanta metro area.  One weak tornado also touched down in Cherokee County.  The line of thunderstorms pushed further southeast with scattered reports of mainly wind damage through the morning and early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey determined that straight-line winds of 80 MPH struck the Floyd-Polk Medical Center in Cedartown.  The winds lifted the vinyl awning off the hospital entrance and pulled some of the metal support beams from the concrete base.  These metal support beams punctured the roof and allowed the rain to enter the building through the holes.  This water caused damage to the interior of the structure.  In addition, some of the debris hit cars parked near the building and damaged six vehicles.  A small portion of the metal roof on a nearby building was peeled back.  A few limbs were down near the medical center and two business signs nearby were damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey determined that a brief EF-0 tornado touched down in Cherokee County.  Although the total path length was three miles, the tornado skipped along the ground with sporadic damage.  Several trees were downed along a path that stretched from 1.75 miles northeast of Waleska to 4.5 miles northeast of Waleska.  In addition to the downed trees, a fence near the starting point of the tornado was damaged when a tree fell on it.|[01/11/14: Tornado #1, County #1-1, EF0, Cherokee, 2014:001].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Steep mid-level lapse rates and an upper level trough moving through combined to produce a few severe storms across the area.  Damaging straight line winds and one isolated, weak tornado occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sporadic tree damage occurred across parts of southern Worth County, with a more concentrated area of damage in the southwest part of the county along and just west of Highway 133. Specifically, the more substantial damage was near the following roads near Hwy 133: Toni Lee, Carlton, Chil Redge, and Liberty Hill. The damage extended approximately 1 mile in a north-south direction and 1 mile in a west-east direction, with most debris blown to the northeast. The circular nature of the area of concentrated damage and consistent nature of the directionality of the damage suggests that it was caused by a small downburst known as a microburst. Much of the damage was consistent with wind gusts of 65-70 mph. However, two residences near the intersection of Carlton Rd and Chil Redge Lane received more significant damage. Several large tree limbs were snapped off. Siding was ripped from the homes, as well as some shingles. A metal shed and metal porch overhang had some material removed and strewn downstream as much as 100 yards. Some wood 2x4s were also blown northeast from the shed and one punctured a screened patio. This sort of damage is consistent with peak gusts in the 80-85 mph range.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Steep mid-level lapse rates and an upper level trough moving through combined to produce a few severe storms across the area.  Damaging straight line winds and one isolated, weak tornado occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fairly narrow corridor of damage points began west of Salem Church Rd about 0.8 mi south of Hwy 82 to the WSW of the city of Tifton. In these areas, mainly tree damage was noted with some large tree limbs snapped and a few trees uprooted. This damage path continued ENE into the vicinity of the Little River, before re-emerging just south of (and along) Hwy 82 near some residences and businesses. The first area affected was a neighborhood of manufactured homes centered around a pond along the south side of Highway 82. Tree damage was noted just south of this with some snapped trees and limbs. One home that was sitting on blocks slid off the blocks and was blown approximately 20 feet. Most of the other homes had only minor damage. A chain link fence was blown down to the north on the south side of the pond, while two billboards mounted on wooden poles were blown down to the west right along Highway 82 (north of the pond). The wooden support poles were snapped. This was indicative of a convergent damage pattern associated with a tornado. Just to the east, near the intersection of Hwy 82 and Marshall Drive, two sheds were flipped and blown back to the southwest - further indicating a convergent damage pattern. Other light debris along Highway 82 was blown in a similar direction. The tornado likely then shifted its motion to slightly south of east, and affected several more businesses north of Marshall Drive and south of Highway 82. Security camera footage from a business in this area showed some swirling rain curtains before very low visibility in strong winds prior to a loss of power. A metal building under construction near the intersection of Carpenter Rd and Marshall Dr was damaged and partially collapsed. Because construction was incomplete, it likely made it easier for the wind to cause the structure to fail. As the tornado crossed Carpenter Rd it likely began to dissipate as damage began to get more sporadic further east by Highway 319. There was some tree damage east of Carpenter Rd, and both the Lowe`s and Wal-Mart to the south of Highway 82 indicated they had some minor roof damage with some AC units moved or tipped over.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wake low developed along the back edge of an area of stratiform rain behind a squall line during the evening hours of March 28th.  Strong wind gusts in excess of 45 mph were measured in Dougherty county as well as Berrien county.  The highest measured wind gust was measured at Alapaha in Berrien county at 51.1 mph at 10:05 pm EDT.  Other wind gusts measured include 46 mph at the Albany airport at 7:34 pm EDT and 47.5 mph at Ducker in western Dougherty county at 7:32 pm EDT.  Numerous trees were reported down in Dougherty and Worth counties with some power outages also reported in Dougherty county.  No trees were reported down in Berrien county.  It is likely that moderate to heavy rain earlier in the day in Dougherty and Worth counties saturated the soil and contributed to the trees falling with winds around 50 mph.  Monetary damage was estimated.

Behind the front, an area of moisture pushed north out of the Gulf on the 28th, spreading precipitation across north and central Georgia.  With the surface cold air in place, the precipitation began as a mix of light sleet and snow across west central Georgia during the mid-morning hours on the 28th, and as the cold air continued to push south, rain across central Georgia changed over to freezing rain, ending as a sleet/snow mix by the evening of the 28th.||The complicating factor for this event was the fact that the high temperature for most of north Georgia occurred early on the 28th, with the strong cold air continuing to funnel in from the north behind the arctic front.  Temperatures fell through the day, and at onset of snow in the Atlanta Metro area, temperatures were in the upper 20s.  Massive gridlock ensued as businesses and schools let out just after noon on the 28th, with thousands of motorists stuck on roads for hours if not overnight.  The significant societal impacts from this event made national news because of the gridlock in the Atlanta Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted north and central Georgia on the 28th.  Snow and sleet began Tuesday afternoon, accumulating to widespread amounts of 1 to 3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the area from the west early on the 11th as an area of cold air damming in place across northeast Georgia began retreating into the Carolinas.  Showers and a few thunderstorms pushed across north Georgia during the early morning hours, but then right after sunrise as the cold front approached the wedge front, thunderstorms intensified and became severe with mainly wind damage reported. Heavy downpours of rain caused localized flash flooding in the Atlanta metro area.  One weak tornado also touched down in Cherokee County.  The line of thunderstorms pushed further southeast with scattered reports of mainly wind damage through the morning and early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported multiple trees down in Irwinton, as well as trees down east of Irwinton along Highway 57.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wake low developed along the back edge of an area of stratiform rain behind a squall line during the evening hours of March 28th.  Strong wind gusts in excess of 45 mph were measured in Dougherty county as well as Berrien county.  The highest measured wind gust was measured at Alapaha in Berrien county at 51.1 mph at 10:05 pm EDT.  Other wind gusts measured include 46 mph at the Albany airport at 7:34 pm EDT and 47.5 mph at Ducker in western Dougherty county at 7:32 pm EDT.  Numerous trees were reported down in Dougherty and Worth counties with some power outages also reported in Dougherty county.  No trees were reported down in Berrien county.  It is likely that moderate to heavy rain earlier in the day in Dougherty and Worth counties saturated the soil and contributed to the trees falling with winds around 50 mph.  Monetary damage was estimated.
A squall line moved across southern Georgia during the afternoon hours of March 16th, producing some reports of straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down along Highway 84 at Patrick Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving through the southeast U.S. and the Tennessee Valley forced abundant gulf moisture into Georgia early Christmas Eve Day. Widespread rain with embedded thunderstorms trained over Crisp County producing 3 to 4 inches of rain in less than ten hours. Isolated flash flooding occurred in the Cordele area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KCSG ASOS reported a 24-hour rainfall total of 2.72 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crisp County emergency manager reported significant overflowing of ditches and small creeks which were flooding several roads in the Cordele area.  A few motorists became stranded in their cars due to the high water. The flood waters surrounded a few homes in the Second Avenue and Third Street section of town.  The water level reached the foundations, but did not enter the houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of light to moderate rain was moving across the area on December 29th in association with a surface cold front. Along the leading edge of the area of rain, a shallow, mini-supercell developed and moved into Lowndes County, Georgia. No lightning was observed with the cell; the tops of the showers were generally below 15,000 feet. As the cell moved over Valdosta, a brief tornado spun up and produced damage up to EF2 intensity on the south side of Valdosta. This was the first tornado to directly affect the city of Valdosta since 1997.||This was an unusual case as atmospheric conditions were not very favorable, though not totally prohibitive, for significant tornadoes. Limited atmospheric instability reduced the overall intensity of showers, despite the one that tracked through Lowndes County, and most of the area just saw a few hours of steady, light to moderate rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first developed just north of Langdales Pond and Old Clyattville Road on the south side of the city of Valdosta. Some of the most severe damage was near the beginning of the track as the tornado impacted Langdale Forest Products along Old  Clyattville Road. Several large metal buildings were severely damaged or destroyed and the damage to those buildings was assigned the EF2 rating that is the maximum for this tornado. Some vehicles parked near those buildings were tossed around 70 yards across the street. Two rail cars nearby were also tipped over. The remainder of the track consisted of EF0 or EF1 damage as the tornado moved east and eventually dissipated near south Patterson Street and Dampier Street. That damage in the remainder of the track was a mixture of damage to buildings, trees, and power lines.  Monetary damage was estimated.||This was the first tornado to directly affect the city of Valdosta since an F1-rated tornado on November 1, 1997.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability and strong shear associated with a deepening upper-level trough and surface low produced scattered thunderstorms across portions of Central Georgia. Several severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and several tornadoes occurred in the afternoon and early evening hours along and behind a warm front lifting north across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upson County 911 Center reported trees and power lines down across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operator reported trees snapped and power lines downed at Emmaus Church Road and Mill Race Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced some urban flash flooding around Valdosta and Albany.  A man was also struck by lightning near Valdosta and subsequently suffered a heart attack and died.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on several roads in Albany due to heavy rain in a short period of time.  The 800 block of Oglethorpe Road was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced some urban flash flooding around Valdosta and Albany.  A man was also struck by lightning near Valdosta and subsequently suffered a heart attack and died.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 57 year old male was struck by lightning at a construction site while sheltering under a pole barn.  He then suffered a heart attack and died as a result of the lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough brought numerous showers and thunderstorms to the tri-state area just before Christmas.  An unseasonably moist environment was in place with TAE reporting its third highest precipitable water value on record for December during this event.  Some flooding and severe weather resulted across southern Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire department reported a brief tornado touchdown in a field.  No structural damage occurred.  There was a small but visible tornadic debris signature on KVAX dual-pol radar as well.  The tornado was rated EF0.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team found that an EF-1 tornado, with maximum wind speeds of 107 MPH, touched down in southwest Monroe County and moved northeast across the county before lifting near Juliette. Extensive tree damage was noted along the path with many trees snapped and uprooted. The tornado reached its peak intensity as it crossed Reedy Creek Road where several large hardwood trees were snapped. A barn collapsed at this location and another had its metal roof peeled off. A shed was destroyed and a tree fell on a house in Smarr. [11/23/14: Tornado #6, County #1-1, EF1, Monroe, 2014:023].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An strong and cold early-season upper-level low dug out of the Ohio Valley into the southern Appalachians bringing an unusually early snowfall to the North Georgia mountains. Light rain showers across North Georgia on Halloween evening began to mix with or change to snow by midnight November 1st. By mid-morning on the 1st many locations in the North Georgia mountains had received generally between a trace and 1 inch of snow, with a few locations in the higher elevations reporting up to 2 inches. Due to warm ground conditions roads across the majority of this area remained clear, with some slushy conditions reported at higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gilmer County 911 Center reported widespread snowfall amounts around 1/2 inch across the county, mainly on grassy and elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very warm and moist air from the Gulf of Mexico prevailed over Georgia in advance of a strong storm system that moved through the eastern United States. During the early morning hours a line of thunderstorms moved through parts of West Central Georgia and continued into the Atlanta metropolitan area and eventually portions of East and Central Georgia. A low-level jet developed on the back side of this line of thunderstorms which was conducive for the development of damaging winds and tornadoes. This line of thunderstorms with heavy rain interacted with a dissipating stationary front early in the morning over North Georgia to produce flash flooding in Dade County in Northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey team found a tornado touched down just southwest of Shiloh in Harris County and traveled northeast for almost 6 miles across northwest Talbot County and southern Meriwether County. A home on the south face of Pine Mountain off Skyline Drive southwest of Manchester lost part of its roof and a large shed was destroyed. A few other homes along the path had minor damage with approximately 100 trees snapped or uprooted. EF1 damage was indicated with maximum wind speeds estimated around 100 MPH and a path width of 200 yards. [10/14/14: Tornado #1, County #1-3, EF1, Harris-Talbot-Meriwether, 2014:011].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm spread into Georgia and South Carolina producing up to 1 inch of ice taking down trees, powerlines, and causing damage to other structures.  Just about everything came to a standstill as federal, state, county, and city governments closed along with schools and businesses.  Power outages were widespread and it took up to four days to restore in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major Ice Storm produced 3/4 to 1 inch of ice and 1 to 2 inches of sleet and snow across Burke County taking down numerous trees and powerlines.  Power outages were widespread across the county affecting most of the population.

During the early morning hours a line of thunderstorms moved through parts of West Central Georgia and continued into the Atlanta metropolitan area and eventually portions of East and Central Georgia. A low-level jet developed on the back side of this line of thunderstorms which was conducive for the development of damaging winds and tornadoes. This line of thunderstorms with heavy rain interacted with a dissipating stationary front early in the morning over North Georgia to produce flash flooding in Dade County in Northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey team found a tornado touched down near Willow Point Parkway in east Cobb County and traveled north northeast into Fulton County north of Timber Ridge Road before lifting along Willeo Road near the Chattahoochee River. EF1 damage was indicated in Cobb County with maximum wind speeds around 95 MPH and a path width of 100 yards. EF0 Damage was indicated after the tornado crossed into Fulton County. Damage was confined mainly to trees snapped or uprooted with some damage to homes from falling trees. [10/14/14: Tornado #5, County #2-2, EF0, Cobb-Fulton, 2014:015].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability and strong shear associated with a deepening upper-level trough and surface low produced scattered thunderstorms across portions of Central Georgia. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team found that an EF-1 tornado, with maximum wind speeds of 100 MPH, touched down in Lamar County near the intersection of State Highway 36 and High Falls Park Road. The tornado tracked northeast nearly 4.5 miles before crossing into Butts County along Bucksnort Road just west of Interstate Highway 75. The tornado caused mainly damage to trees with numerous hardwood and softwood trees snapped or uprooted. [11/23/14: Tornado #8, County #1-3, EF1, Lamar-Butts-Newton, 2014:025].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability and strong shear associated with a deepening upper-level trough and surface low produced scattered thunderstorms across portions of Central Georgia. Several severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and several tornadoes occurred in the afternoon and early evening hours along and behind a warm front lifting north across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team found that the EF-1 tornado that touched down in Lamar  County crossed into southwest Butts County along Bucksnort Road near Interstate Highway 75. The tornado moved northeast across Butts County for just over 16.5 miles before crossing the South River on the upper reaches of Lake Jackson into Newton County. Maximum wind speeds were around 95 MPH with mainly damage to trees. Numerous hardwood and softwood trees were snapped or uprooted. Small outbuildings near the intersection of Cooper Road and State Highway 36 suffered the loss of roof panels. [11/23/14: Tornado #8, County #2-3, EF1, Lamar-Butts-Newton, 2014:025].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability and strong shear associated with a deepening upper-level trough and surface low produced scattered thunderstorms across portions of Central Georgia. Several severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and several tornadoes occurred in the afternoon and early evening hours along and behind a warm front lifting north across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team found that an EF-1 tornado, with maximum wind speeds of 100 MPH, touched down on Highway 26 near Loggins Road where it ripped shingles off of a house and destroyed an above-ground pool. The tornado reached its peak intensity along Pitts Road where a tied-down double-wide mobile home was pushed off of its foundation and had its roof peeled off. On East Flournoy Road a shed was overturned, trees were snapped and a boat was tossed over one hundred feet into a cotton field. Windows were blown out of a house at this location as well. [11/23/14: Tornado #9, County #1-2, EF1, Houston-Pulaski, 2014:026].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability and strong shear associated with a deepening upper-level trough and surface low produced scattered thunderstorms across portions of Central Georgia. Several severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and several tornadoes occurred in the afternoon and early evening hours along and behind a warm front lifting north across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team found that the EF-1 tornado that touched down in Lamar County and crossed Butts County, crossed the South River on the upper reaches of Lake Jackson into Newton County. The tornado only travelled a little over a half mile northeast into Newton County before lifting near the intersection of State Highway 36 and Parker Road. Maximum wind speeds were around 90 MPH with mainly damage to trees. Several hardwood and softwood trees were snapped or uprooted. [11/23/14: Tornado #8, County #3-3, EF1, Lamar-Butts-Newton, 2014:025].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability and strong shear associated with a deepening upper-level trough and surface low produced scattered thunderstorms across portions of Central Georgia. Several severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and several tornadoes occurred in the afternoon and early evening hours along and behind a warm front lifting north across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team found that an EF-1 tornado, with maximum wind speeds of 110 MPH, crossed the Chattahoochee River from Barbour County, Alabama into southwest Stewart County, Georgia southwest of the community of Coffington. The tornado tracked northeast for nearly 14 miles before lifting near the community of Julia along State Highway 39. The tornado mainly caused damage to trees with numerous hardwood and softwood trees either snapped or uprooted. [11/23/14: Tornado #1, County #1-1, EF1, Stewart, 2014:018].

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team found that an EF-1 tornado, with maximum wind speeds of 104 MPH, touched down in central Stewart County just southwest of the city of Lumpkin. The tornado tracked north northeast for less than a mile before lifting causing mainly damage to trees along Green Grove Road. [11/23/14: Tornado #2, County #1-1, EF1, Stewart, 2014:019].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An strong and cold early-season upper-level low dug out of the Ohio Valley into the southern Appalachians bringing an unusually early snowfall to the North Georgia mountains. Light rain showers across North Georgia on Halloween evening began to mix with or change to snow by midnight November 1st. By mid-morning on the 1st many locations in the North Georgia mountains had received generally between a trace and 1 inch of snow, with a few locations in the higher elevations reporting up to 2 inches. Due to warm ground conditions roads across the majority of this area remained clear, with some slushy conditions reported at higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 1 inch of snow on Wilson Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak to moderate instability and strong shear associated with a deepening upper-level trough and surface low produced scattered thunderstorms across portions of Central and East Georgia. A few severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and an isolated tornado occurred in the mid to late morning hours along and behind a warm front lifting north across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Peach County 911 Center reported several trees down southwest of Byron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front advanced slowly north over the state ahead of a deepening upper-level trough digging into the Mississippi Valley, bringing deep moisture and an increasingly unstable air mass to North and Central Georgia. The moist and unstable atmosphere along with persistent divergent upper-level flow, resulted in widespread moderate to heavy rain and isolated severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County Emergency Manager reported several trees and power lines down on Floyd Springs Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team found that an EF-2 tornado, with maximum wind speeds of 130 MPH, touched down in Upson County on the west side of Thomaston around the intersection of State Highway 74 and W. Main Street. The tornado tracked northeast around 8.5 miles mile before crossing into Lamar County near north of Mud Bridge Road and Rocky bottom Road. Numerous hardwood and softwood trees were uprooted or snapped along the path of the tornado. The tornado reached its peak intensity northwest of Thomaston where it moved through a residential subdivision at Del Ray Road and Tyler Lane. A small residential leasing office was destroyed at this location with the exterior walls blown away. [11/23/14: Tornado #5, County #1-2, EF2, Upson-Lamar, 2014:022].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A anomolous winter weather event including sleet, freezing rain and snow flurries occurred on Jan 29th when an area of low pressure deepened offshore of the Fl/Ga Altantic coast and cold and moist air funnelled southward on the west side of the low over southeast Georgia and northeast Florida. An upper level disturbance passed over the shallow cold and moist airmass, with triggered rain mixed with a wintry mix at times during the day on the 29th through the pre-dawn hours of the 30th. Icing on bridges occurred, and many schools across the area closed on the 29th and on the 30th due to the potential for ice freezing on bridges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of sleet, freezing rain and snow flurries occurred across the county. No accumulations reported. Schools were closed on the 29th. Bridges along U.S. Highway 1 over the Altamaha River and the Lanes Bridge on State Road 144 were starting to ice over but were still open at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line of severe storms raced east across the forecast area. Strong shear profiles favored rotating storms. Widespread wind damage occurred, with one confirmed tornado touchdown in Pierce county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low-end EF1 tornado damaged 7 homes, downed power lines, and snapped trees. The tornado touched down about 8 miles north of Blackshear along Walkerville Road around 12:50 pm. Low end EF1 damage was noted along the tornado path as it crossed Georgia State Road 121/15. Including damage to 7 homes, power lines and large trees snapped.  EF0 damage was noted after the tornado crossed Georgia State Road 121/15, with the tornado lifting after an approximate 5.7 mile path near Spotted Farm Road and Songbird Place, or about 14 miles north of Blackshear. The tornado width varied from 60-100 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation associated with a strong upper level disturbance and associated strong cold front developed across the mountains of Northeast Georgia during the early morning hours. As an arctic air mass infiltrated the region behind the front, the precipitation briefly changed to snow in some areas. Although the snow was moderate to heavy at times, a very warm ground and temperatures around 40 degrees resulted in total accumulation of only a dusting up to maybe a couple of inches on grassy surfaces. What little snow did accumulate melted by noon or so.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough brought numerous showers and thunderstorms to the tri-state area just before Christmas.  An unseasonably moist environment was in place with TAE reporting its third highest precipitable water value on record for December during this event.  Some flooding and severe weather resulted across southern Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The airport safety office reported hail around golf ball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough brought numerous showers and thunderstorms to the tri-state area just before Christmas.  An unseasonably moist environment was in place with TAE reporting its third highest precipitable water value on record for December during this event.  Some flooding and severe weather resulted across southern Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The road department placed signs on some roads across the county due to flooding from heavy rain in a short period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving up the GA/SC coast produced freezing rain, sleet, and snow over the CSRA closing schools, businesses, and some highways.  Snow amounts of 1 to 3 inches were common across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced freezing rain, sleet, and snow across Burke County causing hazardous traveling conditions. Ice accumulations were around 1/4 inch with snow/sleet accumulations around an inch.  There was one injury when a truck slid off the road and overturned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced some pea size hail and wind damage around the CSRA taking down trees, street lights, and signs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff Dispatch reported a couple of trees down on the south side of Augusta along with a street light and several signs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced some pea size hail and wind damage around the CSRA taking down trees, street lights, and signs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff Dispatch reported a couple of trees down along Hwy 25 near the Richmond County Line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported total snow accumulations of 1 to 4 inches across the county with the heavier amounts in the northern portions of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced sleet and snow across Columbia County causing hazardous traveling conditions.  Snow/sleet accumulations ranged from 1 to 3 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and surface cold front moved through the region, bringing a round of severe weather across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown into the road a John Rich Road and Highway 310 in western Decatur county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and surface cold front moved through the region, bringing a round of severe weather across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown into the road at Highway 262N and Open Pond Road in Decatur county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and surface cold front moved through the region, bringing a round of severe weather across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down blocking Kennedy Pond Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and surface cold front moved through the region, bringing a round of severe weather across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across 1st Ave SW in Moultrie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and surface cold front moved through the region, bringing a round of severe weather across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down in Valdosta.  One tree fell on a building, one on a car, and one across a road.  No injuries occurred.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong NW flow trailing a cold front combined with low astronomical tides created Blow-Out tides along and offshore of the SE Ga and NE Fl coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blow-out tide levels reached -6.70 feet below NAVD88 at the Sea Camp Dock tidal gage on Cumberland Island. this was about 1.50 ft below predicted low tide levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of dry and sunny weather prior to the 22nd led to dry fuels and increased fire danger across North Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire named Richards Branch resulted in 0.1 acres burned and $1000 in costs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong NW flow trailing a cold front combined with low astronomical tides created Blow-Out tides along and offshore of the SE Ga and NE Fl coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blow-out tide levels reached -7.20 ft below NAVD88 at the St. Simons Island tidal gage. The was about 1.50 to 2.00 ft below the predicted low tide level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong NW flow trailing a cold front combined with low astronomical tides created Blow-Out tides along and offshore of the SE Ga and NE Fl coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blow-out tide levels reached -7.48 ft below NAVD88 at the Brunswick River Tidal Gage in Brunswick. This was about 1.5-2.00 ft below predicted low tide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal line of storms, some severe, tracked acrosss SE Ga and the Suwannee River Valley of NE Fl during the afternoon. The line decreased in coverage and intensity over northeast Florida during the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down with power lines blown down into streets, mainly in the Gardi area. There was a brief power outage in Jesup. A tree was blown down across the road SW of Jesup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal line of storms, some severe, tracked acrosss SE Ga and the Suwannee River Valley of NE Fl during the afternoon. The line decreased in coverage and intensity over northeast Florida during the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down on Dillworth Street that caused multiple fires. A large tree was blown down near the intersection of Seagrove St and West Bryant Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A anomolous winter weather event including sleet, freezing rain and snow flurries occurred on Jan 29th when an area of low pressure deepened offshore of the Fl/Ga Altantic coast and cold and moist air funnelled southward on the west side of the low over southeast Georgia and northeast Florida. An upper level disturbance passed over the shallow cold and moist airmass, with triggered rain mixed with a wintry mix at times during the day on the 29th through the pre-dawn hours of the 30th. Icing on bridges occurred, and many schools across the area closed on the 29th and on the 30th due to the potential for ice freezing on bridges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet was reported in Alma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line of severe storms tracked across SE Ga during the morning hours and then across NE Fl during the early to mid afternoon hours. Severe storms produced widespread wind damage with a few isolated rotating supercells. Sufficient heating near the St. Johns river basin produced elevated surface based instability during the afternoon, and updrafts were greatly enhanced by this forcing. A few isolated pre-squall line cells blossomed over Putnam...St. Johns and Flagler counties in the afternoon and produced large hail. A wall cloud was observed with a rotating storm over Putnam county, and a tornado warning was issued for this cell, however, ground based tornadic damage was not reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty winds were estimated between 55-60 mph near Colesburg Tompkins Road and Highway 17 south of Woodbine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall in pressed across southeast Georgia during the early afternoon and evening hours. Wind damage and heavy rainfall were reported from the stronger storm cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third winter storm to impact the NWS Tallahassee County warning area in five years brought a wintry mix of precipitation to virtually the entire forecast area. The predominant precipitation types were sleet and freezing rain. Total liquid equivalent estimates were greater than a quarter inch across portions of southern Georgia, especially in the counties adjacent to Alabama.  Virtually all of southern Georgia received at least a tenth of an inch of sleet and freezing rain.  Power outages occurred across the area along with treacherous road conditions.||On the morning of January 28th, there was a trough approaching from the west with moist southwest flow and precipitation ahead of it over the Southeast. At the surface, a cold front was stalled across north central Florida and the Gulf of Mexico. However, the deep arctic air was still well north of our region. During the day, the precipitation changed over to snow and/or sleet in places like Atlanta, Birmingham, Macon, and Mobile, as progressively colder air flowed into the region. This caused major traffic problems, especially in Atlanta. ||Late in the afternoon and early evening the rain began to change to freezing rain and sleet in portions of Southeast Alabama, Southwest Georgia, and western portions of the Florida Panhandle. There appeared to be a secondary cold front (a fairly common occurrence when arctic air moves southward) that helped accelerate the advance of the cold air and changeover to freezing/frozen precipitation during the evening. ||During the night (January 28th ��� January 29th) the cold air continued to spread south and east. By the morning of January 29th, many locations in our forecast area had changed to either freezing rain, sleet, or a mix. Much drier air in the mid to upper atmosphere overspread Southeast Alabama and Southwest Georgia. This caused the precipitation to end there during the morning hours. However, light wintry precipitation lingered across the Florida Big Bend throughout the day, as the upper level trough continued to approach from the west. The combination of the unusually cold air and thick clouds prevented many locations from getting above freezing, a very rare occurrence in the Deep South. ||The Wednesday morning surface chart showed the main cold front finally on the move again through South Florida, while high pressure and very cold, dry air continued to infiltrate the Gulf Coast Region. This precipitation tapered off during the evening as the atmosphere dried out.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter-sized hail on Live Oak Road in Ringgold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Steep mid-level lapse rates and moderate low-level shear associated with a deep upper-level trough over the eastern United States produced scattered severe thunderstorms and an isolated tornado across North Georgia during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker County 911 Center reported multiple trees down along State Road 136.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third winter storm to impact the NWS Tallahassee County warning area in five years brought a wintry mix of precipitation to virtually the entire forecast area. The predominant precipitation types were sleet and freezing rain. Total liquid equivalent estimates were greater than a quarter inch across portions of southern Georgia, especially in the counties adjacent to Alabama.  Virtually all of southern Georgia received at least a tenth of an inch of sleet and freezing rain.  Power outages occurred across the area along with treacherous road conditions.||On the morning of January 28th, there was a trough approaching from the west with moist southwest flow and precipitation ahead of it over the Southeast. At the surface, a cold front was stalled across north central Florida and the Gulf of Mexico. However, the deep arctic air was still well north of our region. During the day, the precipitation changed over to snow and/or sleet in places like Atlanta, Birmingham, Macon, and Mobile, as progressively colder air flowed into the region. This caused major traffic problems, especially in Atlanta. ||Late in the afternoon and early evening the rain began to change to freezing rain and sleet in portions of Southeast Alabama, Southwest Georgia, and western portions of the Florida Panhandle. There appeared to be a secondary cold front (a fairly common occurrence when arctic air moves southward) that helped accelerate the advance of the cold air and changeover to freezing/frozen precipitation during the evening. ||During the night (January 28th ��� January 29th) the cold air continued to spread south and east. By the morning of January 29th, many locations in our forecast area had changed to either freezing rain, sleet, or a mix. Much drier air in the mid to upper atmosphere overspread Southeast Alabama and Southwest Georgia. This caused the precipitation to end there during the morning hours. However, light wintry precipitation lingered across the Florida Big Bend throughout the day, as the upper level trough continued to approach from the west. The combination of the unusually cold air and thick clouds prevented many locations from getting above freezing, a very rare occurrence in the Deep South. ||The Wednesday morning surface chart showed the main cold front finally on the move again through South Florida, while high pressure and very cold, dry air continued to infiltrate the Gulf Coast Region. This precipitation tapered off during the evening as the atmosphere dried out.

There appeared to be a secondary cold front (a fairly common occurrence when arctic air moves southward) that helped accelerate the advance of the cold air and changeover to freezing/frozen precipitation during the evening. ||During the night (January 28th ��� January 29th) the cold air continued to spread south and east. By the morning of January 29th, many locations in our forecast area had changed to either freezing rain, sleet, or a mix. Much drier air in the mid to upper atmosphere overspread Southeast Alabama and Southwest Georgia. This caused the precipitation to end there during the morning hours. However, light wintry precipitation lingered across the Florida Big Bend throughout the day, as the upper level trough continued to approach from the west. The combination of the unusually cold air and thick clouds prevented many locations from getting above freezing, a very rare occurrence in the Deep South. ||The Wednesday morning surface chart showed the main cold front finally on the move again through South Florida, while high pressure and very cold, dry air continued to infiltrate the Gulf Coast Region. This precipitation tapered off during the evening as the atmosphere dried out.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An irrigation pivot was flipped along Isabella-Nashville Road to the southwest of the beginning of the tornado.  More concentrated tree damage was located just east along Salem Church Road as well.  Further wind damage was noted in the city of Tifton, mainly to the south and southeast of the tornado damage path. In addition to being slightly south of the trajectory of the tornado, the damage in this area was not continuous, more sporadic, and was in a consistent direction (blown in a general NE direction). Therefore, it was determined that this was likely due to NON-tornadic winds. The damage was most notable from near Old Omega Rd and I-75 east towards the vicinity of Beech Street south of downtown. The damage in these areas mostly consisted of tree limbs snapped off or trees uprooted. There was some metal roofing taken off a building along Beech street as well. A couple street signs were blown down.  Winds were estimated at 65-75 mph.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line of severe storms tracked across SE Ga during the morning hours and then across NE Fl during the early to mid afternoon hours. Severe storms produced widespread wind damage with a few isolated rotating supercells. Sufficient heating near the St. Johns river basin produced elevated surface based instability during the afternoon, and updrafts were greatly enhanced by this forcing. A few isolated pre-squall line cells blossomed over Putnam...St. Johns and Flagler counties in the afternoon and produced large hail. A wall cloud was observed with a rotating storm over Putnam county, and a tornado warning was issued for this cell, however, ground based tornadic damage was not reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds blew down a large tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front pushed through the Southeast on the 27th, leaving very cold air in its wake.  Behind the front, an area of moisture pushed north out of the Gulf on the 28th, spreading precipitation across north and central Georgia.  With the surface cold air in place, the precipitation began as a mix of light sleet and snow across west central Georgia during the mid-morning hours on the 28th, and as the cold air continued to push south, rain across central Georgia changed over to freezing rain, ending as a sleet/snow mix by the evening of the 28th.||The complicating factor for this event was the fact that the high temperature for most of north Georgia occurred early on the 28th, with the strong cold air continuing to funnel in from the north behind the arctic front.  Temperatures fell through the day, and at onset of snow in the Atlanta Metro area, temperatures were in the upper 20s.  Massive gridlock ensued as businesses and schools let out just after noon on the 28th, with thousands of motorists stuck on roads for hours if not overnight.  The significant societal impacts from this event made national news because of the gridlock in the Atlanta Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted north and central Georgia on the 28th.  Snow and sleet began Tuesday afternoon, accumulating to widespread amounts of 1 to 3 inches of snow.  Across the Metro Atlanta area, thousands of motorists were trapped on icy roads for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed across the Southeast on the 20th and 21st, with a QLCS (quasi-linear convective system) moving into northwest Georgia just after midnight on the 21st.  Scattered reports of wind damage were received across north Georgia through the early morning hours.  By mid-morning, the QLCS had pushed into central Georgia and intensified as instability increased, with a high-end EF2 tornado touching down in Laurens and Johnson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter sized hail in Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic high pressure area was moving toward the Southeast on the 23rd.  Very weak forcing aloft allowed for a few showers to develop across central Georgia during the afternoon, but the rest of the area remained dry.  While critical fire weather conditions were not met, a wildfire did still start in north Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Hickey burned 5.9 acres and resulted in $15,000 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rain moved across central and portions of north Georgia on the 26th, with a light wintry mix over the mountains.  However, a cooler and drier arimass did move into north Georgia during the afternoon bringing dangerous fire weather conditions to the mountain areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Rocky Point burned 0.5 acres and resulted in $300 in damages.

With the surface cold air in place, the precipitation began as a mix of light sleet and snow across west central Georgia during the mid-morning hours on the 28th, and as the cold air continued to push south, rain across central Georgia changed over to freezing rain, ending as a sleet/snow mix by the evening of the 28th.||The complicating factor for this event was the fact that the high temperature for most of north Georgia occurred early on the 28th, with the strong cold air continuing to funnel in from the north behind the arctic front.  Temperatures fell through the day, and at onset of snow in the Atlanta Metro area, temperatures were in the upper 20s.  Massive gridlock ensued as businesses and schools let out just after noon on the 28th, with thousands of motorists stuck on roads for hours if not overnight.  The significant societal impacts from this event made national news because of the gridlock in the Atlanta Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted north and central Georgia on the 28th.  Snow and sleet began Tuesday afternoon, accumulating to widespread amounts of 1 to 3 inches of snow.

With the surface cold air in place, the precipitation began as a mix of light sleet and snow across west central Georgia during the mid-morning hours on the 28th, and as the cold air continued to push south, rain across central Georgia changed over to freezing rain, ending as a sleet/snow mix by the evening of the 28th.||The complicating factor for this event was the fact that the high temperature for most of north Georgia occurred early on the 28th, with the strong cold air continuing to funnel in from the north behind the arctic front.  Temperatures fell through the day, and at onset of snow in the Atlanta Metro area, temperatures were in the upper 20s.  Massive gridlock ensued as businesses and schools let out just after noon on the 28th, with thousands of motorists stuck on roads for hours if not overnight.  The significant societal impacts from this event made national news because of the gridlock in the Atlanta Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted north and central Georgia on the 28th.  Snow and sleet began Tuesday afternoon, accumulating to widespread amounts of 1 to 3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dangerous fire weather conditions were in place across north Georgia for the first couple of days in April due to dry low level conditions and low fuel moisture values.  A few wildfires developed, including a significant one in Floyd County with over 100 acres burned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a wildfire of unknown origin labeled Crackerneck burned 117 acres and resulted in $11,000 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold airmass was in place on the 25th, cold enough to support some dry snow in the Georgia mountains.  Temperatures warmed on the 26th as weak high pressure moved over the area.  High fire danger conditions were in place both on the 25th and 26th, based on low fuel moistures and a combination of strong winds on the 25th and low relative humidities on the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled 3300 Fire burned 3.75 acres and resulted in $800 in damages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Mack White Gap burned 47 acres and resulted in $15,000 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe storm developed near Valdosta and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe trees were blown down, including a few on houses.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent heavy rainfall across Brooks county led to flash flooding with road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed across the county due to water over the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed across the Southeast on the 20th and 21st, with a QLCS (quasi-linear convective system) moving into northwest Georgia just after midnight on the 21st.  Scattered reports of wind damage were received across north Georgia through the early morning hours.  By mid-morning, the QLCS had pushed into central Georgia and intensified as instability increased, with a high-end EF2 tornado touching down in Laurens and Johnson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Macon broadcast media relayed a report of a tree down along Sugarloaf Circle.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey determined that straight-line winds snapped two trees and uprooted five other, with several branches down in the area as well.

Temperatures fell through the day, and at onset of snow in the Atlanta Metro area, temperatures were in the upper 20s.  Massive gridlock ensued as businesses and schools let out just after noon on the 28th, with thousands of motorists stuck on roads for hours if not overnight.  The significant societal impacts from this event made national news because of the gridlock in the Atlanta Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted north and central Georgia on the 28th.  Snow and sleet began Tuesday afternoon, accumulating to widespread amounts of 1 to 3 inches of snow, up to a half inch of sleet, and up to a quarter of an inch of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper ridge that had dominated the area for the past several days began shifting to the west on the 21st, leaving the Southeast in northwest flow aloft. Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed during the day, and several of these became severe with damaging winds reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pickens County 911 Center reported a few trees down near Lower Dowda Mill Road and Bethany Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry high pressure continued across the Southeast with above-normal temperatures.  Moisture was on the increase ahead of an approaching front, keeping dangerous fire weather conditions at bay, but a wildfire was still able to develop in north Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through the area on the 18th.  Instability increased across central Georgia during the afternoon hours, with several showers and thunderstorms developing.  A few of these thunderstorms became severe with both large hail and damaging winds reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montgomery County 911 Center reported that large quarter to golf ball sized hail fell about three miles south of Mount Vernon along Thompson Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge over the Mississippi River valley led to northwest flow aloft over the Southeast.  Daytime heating provided plenty of instability, and a weak surface boundary in place oriented northwest to southeast provided focus for numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop.  Many of these storms became severe with large hail and damaging winds reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported golf ball sized hail along Yona Homer Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of very hot temperatures and increased instability ahead of a frontal zone over Georgia as well as increased bulk shear spawned a severe storm over Atkinson county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto power lines along Talmadge McKinnon Road. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire broke out over Bacon county after a few days of hot temperatures an heat advisories.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 200 firefighters were battling a 600 acre wildfire located just north of Alma along Bennett Still Highway. Crews reported that the fire was 80 percent contained. Twelve homes were threatened. One forest service crew member was taken to the hospital due to heat related illness.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow developed over northeast Georgia during the afternoon and continued into the evening before tapering off. The snow initially melted on roads. However, air temperatures rapidly cooling into the 20s caused many roads to subsequently freeze. Although snowfall totals were light (1 to 2 inches or less in most areas), the slick roads caused hundreds of traffic accidents.

However, air temperatures rapidly cooling into the 20s caused many roads to subsequently freeze. Although snowfall totals were light (1 to 2 inches or less in most areas), the slick roads caused hundreds of traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow developed over northeast Georgia during the afternoon and continued into the evening before tapering off. The snow initially melted on roads. However, air temperatures rapidly cooling into the 20s caused many roads to subsequently freeze. Although snowfall totals were light (1 to 2 inches or less in most areas), the slick roads caused hundreds of traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the a very unstable atmosphere, the presence of a weak surface trough and the passing of a Mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV) produced numerous showers and thunderstorms. A line of stationary thunderstorms produced between 4-10 inches of rain across Chatham County, which resulted in flash flooding. KSAV observed the wettest June day on record since observations began in 1871.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding along with numerous road closures were reported in parts of Garden City, Pooler, Port Wentworth and at the Savannah Airport in Georgia. Water levels were reported being over tires, flooding was observed around some businesses and tow trucks were used to relocate vehicles from flooded locations. Flood waters also washed out a section of the CSX rail line near Highway 307 and Gulf Stream Road. Total costs were generally estimated to be around 15.0K.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the a very unstable atmosphere, the presence of a weak surface trough and the passing of a Mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV) produced numerous showers and thunderstorms. A line of stationary thunderstorms produced between 4-10 inches of rain across Chatham County, which resulted in flash flooding. KSAV observed the wettest June day on record since observations began in 1871.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Savannah Airport ASOS measured 6.65 inches of rainfall for the day. This is the wettest June day on record at the airport since records began in 1871. This surpassed the previous record of 6.60 inches set in June 29, 1999.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very warm and moist air from the Gulf of Mexico prevailed over Georgia in advance of a strong storm system that moved through the eastern United States. During the early morning hours a line of thunderstorms moved through parts of West Central Georgia and continued into the Atlanta metropolitan area and eventually portions of East and Central Georgia. A low-level jet developed on the back side of this line of thunderstorms which was conducive for the development of damaging winds and tornadoes. This line of thunderstorms with heavy rain interacted with a dissipating stationary front early in the morning over North Georgia to produce flash flooding in Dade County in Northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey team found a tornado touched down just southwest of Shiloh in Harris County and traveled northeast for almost 6 miles across northwest Talbot County and southern Meriwether County. A home on the south face of Pine Mountain off Skyline Drive southwest of Manchester lost part of its roof and a large shed was destroyed. A few other homes along the path had minor damage with approximately 100 trees snapped or uprooted. EF1 damage was indicated with maximum wind speeds estimated around 100 MPH and a path width of 200 yards. [10/14/14: Tornado #1, County #2-3, EF1, Harris-Talbot-Meriwether, 2014:011].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Steep mid-level lapse rates and moderate low-level shear associated with a deep upper-level trough over the eastern United States produced scattered severe thunderstorms and an isolated tornado across North Georgia during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey team found that an EF1 tornado touched down just north of Ringgold, GA. The tornado was on the ground between a quarter and a third of a mile, however dozens of trees were snapped or uprooted in the Calloway Farms subdivision. Seven homes sustained damage with one home uninhabitable. One home lost a section of its roof and an attic wall and another home had the garage doors blown out. [10/06/14: Tornado#1, County#1-1, EF1, Catoosa, 2014:010}.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over the Tennessee Valley separated very warm and moist air from the Gulf of Mexico over Georgia from cooler air over Kentucky. A series of weak upper-level short waves combined with strong diurnal heating in the afternoon and early evening to produce scattered severe thunderstorms across North Georgia. Some of the thunderstorms produced heavy rain of 1.5 to 2 inches in less than two hours as they moved to the east resulting in isolated flash flooding near Ellijay in North Central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gilmer County Deputy Emergency Manager reported a low lying area of Cox Creek around 1 mile northwest of Ellijay was flooding and beginning to surround two homes near the intersection of Chatsworth Highway and Tower Road. Minor flooding of the yards and driveways occurred, but no water entered the homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very warm and moist air from the Gulf of Mexico prevailed over Georgia in advance of a strong storm system that moved through the eastern United States. During the early morning hours a line of thunderstorms moved through parts of West Central Georgia and continued into the Atlanta metropolitan area and eventually portions of East and Central Georgia. A low-level jet developed on the back side of this line of thunderstorms which was conducive for the development of damaging winds and tornadoes. This line of thunderstorms with heavy rain interacted with a dissipating stationary front early in the morning over North Georgia to produce flash flooding in Dade County in Northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Manager reported multiple trees down on Ben Hill Road and Stone Road. Power lines were reported down on Fairburn Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system enhanced the pressure gradient and led to gusty winds over the southeast. Previous rains along with these gusty winds contributed to a tree falling during the early overnight period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meandering frontal zone over southeast Georgia combined with a broad low amplitude trough provided enough lift and destabilization to trigger a severe storm which produced large hail over inland SE Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail estimated at ping pong ball size was reported along Old Spivey Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked from the Gulf of Mexico to off the southeast coast, producing widespread precipitation across southeast Georgia. A wedge of high pressure provided shallow, cold air, supporting the development of freezing rain at many locations away from the immediate coast. Across many inland areas, a major ice storm occurred with one to three quarters of an inch of ice accumulation and even a few one inch accumulation amounts reported. Widespread trees, large tree limbs, and power lines were reported down due to ice. Many trees were down and blocking area roadways. Widespread, long duration power outages also occurred especially across inland areas of Jenkins, Bulloch, and Screven counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total ice accumulations across Jenkins County ranged up to three quarters of an inch. Widespread trees and large tree limbs were reported down due to the ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level long wave trough with a surface area of low pressure and cold front moved through the southeast states.  Thunderstorms developed ahead of the front, some of which became severe and produced wind damage.  One tornado was confirmed in Irwin county.  Heavy rainfall also led to some areas of flooding with widespread amounts of 3 to 5 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Old Valley Grove Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad closed upper low over the Midwest dropped Southeast on the 28th and 29th, with several shortwaves rotating around the larger low.  Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms developed across the area as a result.  A strong cold front moved through the Southeast on the 29th, finally pushing the system out of the Southeast, but not before scattered severe thunderstorms and isolated flash flooding occurred from the widespread 2 to 6 inches of rain that fell over northwest Georgia.  Two weak tornadoes also occurred with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett County Emergency Manager reported that 16 trees and 18 powerlines were blown down.  Six of the trees fell on houses.  Damage was spread throughout the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down in Irwin county south of Ocilla and produced mainly EF0 damage along its path, largely in the form of uprooted trees with some smaller trees snapped.  The tornado was at its strongest near the end of its damage path where EF1 damage was noted.  A mobile home was knocked several feet off its foundation, but remained upright.  Most of the roof was torn off.  A large hardwood tree was snapped in half and many others were uprooted.  Several out buildings were destroyed and shingles were stripped from the roof of one home.  Peak winds were estimated around 105 mph.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering area of cold air damming was in place across northeast Georgia as a strong low over the Gulf lifted northeast, aided potent upper shortwave pushing through the Tennessee Valleys.  Widespread rain with embedded thunderstorms developed during the day, producing 2 to 6 inches of rain over north and central Georgia.  This rain resulted in isolated flash flooding and subsequent widespread areal and river flooding. Isolated severe thunderstorms developed as well, including a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Carroll County Emergency Manager reported around ten roads closed due to flooding from nearby creeks or streams. Two of the roads had structural damage needing repairs. The area primarily affected was from Carrollton to Whitesburg to Roopville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey determined that straight-line winds from a bow echo resulted in scattered wind damage across southern Toombs County from Greys Landing Road to Charlie Hart Road. An unanchored single-wide mobile home was rolled and destroyed.  Wind gusts were estimated to be around 70 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad closed upper low over the Midwest dropped Southeast on the 28th and 29th, with several shortwaves rotating around the larger low.  Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms developed across the area as a result.  A strong cold front moved through the Southeast on the 29th, finally pushing the system out of the Southeast, but not before scattered severe thunderstorms and isolated flash flooding occurred from the widespread 2 to 6 inches of rain that fell over northwest Georgia.  Two weak tornadoes also occurred with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker County emergency manager reported in the Cedar Grove area that several small streams and branches in the upper reach of the West Chickamauga Creek basin overflowed their banks and covered some roads up to one foot deep. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level long wave trough with a surface area of low pressure and cold front moved through the southeast states.  Thunderstorms developed ahead of the front, some of which became severe and produced wind damage.  One tornado was confirmed in Irwin county.  Heavy rainfall also led to some areas of flooding with widespread amounts of 3 to 5 inches reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sheds were blown away near Mystic.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong but shallow arctic cold front pushed through the region early in the day on 1/28/14. By that evening, a strong cold air damming regime prevailed as northerly surface winds pushed temperatures to around freezing across nearly all of southeast Georgia. Above the shallow freezing layer, a prominent elevated warm layer developed which resulted in the predominant precipitation type being freezing rain for the event. Strong synoptic forcing produced widespread precipitation through the event. Storm total ice accumulations ranged up to one half of an inch especially inland away from the coast. There were even a few isolated one inch amounts reported. Total snow and sleet accumulations were less than an inch. The unusually high amounts of ice accretion caused widespread major travel impacts with several bridge being closed due to ice. Furthermore, temperatures remained near or below freezing at many locations through 1/31/14, which slowed the melting of ice and prolonged travel impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reports sleet and freezing rain in the Townsend area. No accumulations observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a pre-frontal squall line, the Storm Prediction Center issued a Tornado Watch for much of the region due to a favorable combination of kinematic and thermodynamic parameters. An outflow boundary advanced south into the I-16 corridor and just north of Beaufort/Charleston, pushing surface based instability into southern/coastal counties of the forecast area. A bowing segment interacted with this boundary and produced a few straight-line wind damage events from Tattnall County east to Chatham County. Another bowing segment brushed McIntosh County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hinesville Police Department reported a utility pole struck by lightning at the intersection of Horne Drive and Oglethorpe Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front blasted through northeast Georgia during the morning of the 6th, bringing gusty winds and the coldest air mass to have affected the region since 1994. By early evening, winds of 10 to 20 mph, with stronger gusts combined with temperatures falling into the 20s and teens to produce wind chill values below 0 across the Georgia. Wind chills across the higher elevations likely reached -15 or lower briefly on the night of the 6th. Although wind gradually diminished overnight, low temperatures on the 7th ranged from a little below zero above 3000 feet to the single digits across the Piedmont and foothills.
