EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper ridge continued to dominate the area, but a weak boundary was in place across north Georgia, which provided a focus for thunderstorms to develop.  One became severe with damaging winds reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gordon County 911 Center reported that four trees were blown down on roads including Highway 411 and Highway 53 in the eastern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong but shallow arctic cold front pushed through the region early in the day on 1/28/14. By that evening, a strong cold air damming regime prevailed as northerly surface winds pushed temperatures to around freezing across nearly all of southeast Georgia. Above the shallow freezing layer, a prominent elevated warm layer developed which resulted in the predominant precipitation type being freezing rain for the event. Strong synoptic forcing produced widespread precipitation through the event. Storm total ice accumulations ranged up to one half of an inch especially inland away from the coast. There were even a few isolated one inch amounts reported. Total snow and sleet accumulations were less than an inch. The unusually high amounts of ice accretion caused widespread major travel impacts with several bridge being closed due to ice. Furthermore, temperatures remained near or below freezing at many locations through 1/31/14, which slowed the melting of ice and prolonged travel impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters observed one quarter to three tenths of an inch of ice accumulation across the northern half of the county. Light snow also fell but did not result in significant accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough moved through the Southeast late on the 7th and early on the 8th, forming a squall line that pushed across central Georgia during the very early morning hours.  A weak tornado formed in Troup County, and with a few reports of wind damage, mainly downed trees, as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey team determined that an overnight squall line interacted with an outflow boundary to produce a brief EF-1 tornado just east of West Point Lake in the Heritage Hills subdivision, where a couple of trees were snapped and uprooted at touchdown.  The tornado continued to the southeast where additional trees were snapped and uprooted.  One tree fell on three cars, crushing them.  The tornado continued to the next block where it destroyed a greenhouse.  The tornado lifted near Georgia Highway 29.  Overall there were 50 large trees downed, several of which were on houses and vehicles.  A couple of additional houses had shingle and siding damage as well.|[06/08/14: Tornado #1, County #1-1, EF1, Troup, 2014:009].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper ridge remained in place on the 20th, but a very moist airmass was in place and a shortwave was approaching the Ohio Valley for the afternoon.  Several showers and thunderstorms developed across the area, and a couple of them became severe with mainly damaging winds reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Catoosa County 911 Center reported two trees down north of Shookville.

Several showers and thunderstorms developed across the area, and a couple of them became severe with mainly damaging winds reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County Emergency Manager reported six trees down in the Rome area.  One tree fell on a car with two occupants; one occupant was injured.

While the system itself weakened, it helped to trigger scattered to widespread showers and thunderstorms, several of which became severe with mainly wind damage noted across mainly north Georgia.  One weak tornado also occurred in Gilmer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Laurens County 911 Center reported that several trees and power lines were blown down.

The upper ridge continued in place across the Southeast, with a weak shortwave pushing down the northwest flow aloft.  Once again, scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed and a few of these became severe with downed trees reported.  Additionally, thunderstorms produced around two inches of rain in less than two hours in south central Gilmer County.  The heavy rain fell over wet ground from previous rain the day before.  Rapid runoff caused isolated flash flooding in that area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gilmer County Deputy Emergency Manager reported flood waters from a stream were surrounding and flowing under a home on Shamrock Farm Road around 6 miles south to southeast of Ellijay. Three persons in the home were rescued by first responders in a boat. Some erosion occurred to the rock road to the house. The flood waters did not enter the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed in the afternoon hours across southeast Georgia and moved eastward into southeast South Carolina. These thunderstorms developed in a warm and moist atmosphere well ahead of a cold front to the west. Some of the thunderstorms organized into multi-cell clusters and numerous reports of wind damage across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bulloch County communications center reported trees down in the Lakeview area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant, deep moisture and a series of short waves moving through the region in a generally zonal mid and upper-level flow pattern produced isolated to scattered reports of thunderstorm wind damage across North and Central Georgia each day, mainly during the afternoon and evening hours when instability was maximized due to diurnal heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were uprooted along Bryan Street and Park Avenue. The large and mature trees caused damage to at least two houses and a dozen cars and also ruptured a gas line and snapped power poles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fulton County Emergency Manager reported several trees down and power outages in the Grant Park area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Center reported two trees down along highway 49.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Center reported a tree down on Lee Road and powerlines down on Skyview Drive in Lithia Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough continued to dominate the eastern portion of the country on the 20th, but with ridging beginning to build in aloft. A shortwave pushed across the Southeast late in the evening, sparking numerous showers and thunderstorms.  Nearly stationary thunderstorms produced heavy rain over portions of Heard and Troup Counties. Rainfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches occurred primarily just southeast of Franklin. This caused flash flooding on streams and creeks flowing into the Chattahoochee River in southeast Heard County. The flood waters caused the pool elevation on West Point Lake a few miles downstream to rise around one and a half feet.  In this same storm, lightning struck a house, resulting in a fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Heard County Park Ranger reported flash flooding at the Brush Creek Park by the Chattahoochee River around 5 miles south to southeast of Franklin. Most of the campers had already left in the evening. The Heard County Emergency Manager confirmed through the Public Works Department that several dirt and rock roads east and south of Franklin had significant erosion damage and will need regrading. No culvert pipes were washed out.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Heard County Emergency Manager reported that a house around two miles southeast of Franklin on Pike Road had major damage from a fire caused by a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms moving from the east toward the west developed near the Blue Ridge during the late evening of the 11th, then briefly became severe around midnight, producing a downburst in Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported multiple trees and power lines blown down in and around Clayton, including on Main St and Savannah St. One tree fell on a vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe storm in extreme southeast Richmond county produced golf ball size hail about 7 miles east northeast of Mc Bean.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported golf ball size hail at hunt club about 7 miles east northeast of Mc Bean.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed in the afternoon hours across southeast Georgia. These thunderstorms developed along and just ahead of a unseasonably strong cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Candler County communications center reported trees down on Rosemary Church Road just off of Highway 121.  Road crews had to clear the trees from the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second major winter storm in as many weeks hit north and central Georgia on February 12th.  An area of extremely strong cold air damming moved down the Eastern Seaboard and into north Georgia on the 12th.  Southwest flow aloft allowed significant moisture to move across the Southeast, with a disturbance pushing across the southern tier of the country.  A surface low developed in the northern Gulf on the 12th, pushing into extreme Southeast Georgia on the 13th.  Moisture overspread the area in advance of the surface low and with cold surface temperatures in place from the cold air damming, another significant winter storm hit the area.||This storm was different from the storm two weeks prior for two main reasons: firstly, residents across north and central Georgia were well-prepared for the event, and secondly, this storm brought with it catastrophic accumulations of ice along the Interstate 20 corridor east of Atlanta towards Athens.  Significant amounts of snow fell in north Georgia as well.  The crippling nature of this winter storm, unlike the first, was not necessarily due to the societal impacts but rather to the significant accumulations of ice and snow and resulting widespread power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted north and portions of central Georgia on Tuesday the 11th and Wednesday the 12th. For areas south of the Atlanta Metropolitan area and into central Georgia, the event began Wednesday morning the 12th.  Rain mixed with and changed over to freezing rain through the morning hours, resulting in catastrophic ice accretions of a half to one inch of ice, with localized higher amounts, especially along the Interstate 20 corridor.  Thousands of trees were downed and widespread power outages were reported, with some customers without power for days.  The event ended as a round of light snow Wednesday evening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted north and portions of central Georgia on Tuesday the 11th and Wednesday the 12th.  Light snow began across north Georgia early Tuesday morning with the first round of wintry precipitation, followed by a brief lull Tuesday night, and a second, more significant, round of snow, sleet, and freezing rain on Wednesday morning, ending finally as light snow Wednesday evening.  Overall across the Metropolitan Atlanta counties and areas east (along and just south of Interstate 85) and west (along Interstate 20), sleet accumulations of 0.25 to 0.75 inches, freezing rain accumulations of 0.1 to 0.25 inches, and snowfall accumulations of 1 to 2 inches were reported.

Most of this fell with the first round of precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed across the Southeast on the 20th and 21st, with a QLCS (quasi-linear convective system) moving into northwest Georgia just after midnight on the 21st.  Scattered reports of wind damage were received across north Georgia through the early morning hours.  By mid-morning, the QLCS had pushed into central Georgia and intensified as instability increased, with a high-end EF2 tornado touching down in Laurens and Johnson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS site KMGE in Marietta measured a 50 kt gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Miller type-A low pressure system moved up along the South Carolina coast bringing widespread snow, which by late morning began to change to sleet and freezing rain.  This transition zone gradually spread north throughout the day and into the evening, although precipitation periodically changed back to snow later in the evening and during the overnight. Snowfall and sleet accumulation generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the area, with locally higher amounts reported in the high elevations. Meanwhile, some areas saw light ice accretion of around a tenth of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed across the Southeast on the 20th and 21st, with a QLCS (quasi-linear convective system) moving into northwest Georgia just after midnight on the 21st.  Scattered reports of wind damage were received across north Georgia through the early morning hours.  By mid-morning, the QLCS had pushed into central Georgia and intensified as instability increased, with a high-end EF2 tornado touching down in Laurens and Johnson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey team determined that an EF2 tornado with maximum wind speeds of 124 MPH touched down just east of the airport in Dublin, where a house was destroyed.  The tornado then continued east through North Dublin where damage was consistent with 100-110 MPH winds.  In Dublin, 59 houses were damaged and 300-500 trees were downed as a result of this QLCS tornado.  The tornado weakened as it moved through Laurens County and finally into Johnson County.|[02/21/14: Tornado #1, County #1-2, EF2, Laurens-Johnson, 2014:002].

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey team determined that the tornado that began in Laurens County continued into Johnson County.  A mobile home was lifted off its foundation while another had its porch blown off by the storm.  Doors of the Piney  Mountain Fire Station were also blown off by the tornado.  The tornado eventually dissipated northeast of the intersection between Highway 15/78 and Snell Bridge Road.|[02/21/14: Tornado #1, County #2-2, EF2, Laurens-Johnson, 2014:002].

Rain mixed with and changed over to freezing rain through the morning hours, resulting in catastrophic ice accretions of a half to one inch of ice, with localized higher amounts, especially along the Interstate 20 corridor.  Thousands of trees were downed and widespread power outages were reported, with some customers without power for days.  The event ended as a round of light snow Wednesday evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second major winter storm in as many weeks hit north and central Georgia on February 12th.  An area of extremely strong cold air damming moved down the Eastern Seaboard and into north Georgia on the 12th.  Southwest flow aloft allowed significant moisture to move across the Southeast, with a disturbance pushing across the southern tier of the country.  A surface low developed in the northern Gulf on the 12th, pushing into extreme Southeast Georgia on the 13th.  Moisture overspread the area in advance of the surface low and with cold surface temperatures in place from the cold air damming, another significant winter storm hit the area.||This storm was different from the storm two weeks prior for two main reasons: firstly, residents across north and central Georgia were well-prepared for the event, and secondly, this storm brought with it catastrophic accumulations of ice along the Interstate 20 corridor east of Atlanta towards Athens.  Significant amounts of snow fell in north Georgia as well.  The crippling nature of this winter storm, unlike the first, was not necessarily due to the societal impacts but rather to the significant accumulations of ice and snow and resulting widespread power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted north and portions of central Georgia on Tuesday the 11th and Wednesday the 12th.  Snow began across north Georgia early Tuesday morning with the first round of wintry precipitation, followed by a brief lull Tuesday night, and a second round of snow on Wednesday morning.  Overall, heavy snow across extreme north Georgia accumulated to widespread amounts of 4 to 8 inches, with isolated higher reports up to 11 inches.  Most of this fell with the first round of precipitation.

Light snow began across north Georgia early Tuesday morning with the first round of wintry precipitation, followed by a brief lull Tuesday night, and a second, more significant, round of snow, sleet, and freezing rain on Wednesday morning, ending finally as light snow Wednesday evening.  Overall across the Metropolitan Atlanta counties and areas east (along and just south of Interstate 85) and west (along Interstate 20), sleet accumulations of 0.25 to 0.75 inches, freezing rain accumulations of 0.1 to 0.25 inches, and snowfall accumulations of 1 to 2 inches were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted north and portions of central Georgia on Tuesday the 11th and Wednesday the 12th. For areas south of the Atlanta Metropolitan area and into central Georgia, the event began Wednesday morning the 12th.  Rain mixed with and changed over to freezing rain through the morning hours, resulting in catastrophic ice accretions of a half to one inch of ice, with localized higher amounts, especially along the Interstate 20 corridor.  Thousands of trees were downed and widespread power outages were reported, with some customers without power for days.  The event ended as a round of light snow Wednesday evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Miller type-A low pressure system moved up along the South Carolina coast bringing widespread snow, which by late morning began to change to sleet and freezing rain.  This transition zone gradually spread north throughout the day and into the evening, although precipitation periodically changed back to snow later in the evening and during the overnight. Snowfall and sleet accumulation generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the area, with locally higher amounts reported in the high elevations. Meanwhile, some areas saw light ice accretion of around a tenth of an inch.

Widespread power outages were reported, with some customers without power for over two days.  The event ended as a round of light snow Wednesday evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line of severe storms tracked across SE Ga during the morning hours and then across NE Fl during the early to mid afternoon hours. Severe storms produced widespread wind damage with a few isolated rotating supercells. Sufficient heating near the St. Johns river basin produced elevated surface based instability during the afternoon, and updrafts were greatly enhanced by this forcing. A few isolated pre-squall line cells blossomed over Putnam...St. Johns and Flagler counties in the afternoon and produced large hail. A wall cloud was observed with a rotating storm over Putnam county, and a tornado warning was issued for this cell, however, ground based tornadic damage was not reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees were blown down onto a building which caused damage near U.S. Highway 84 about 1 mile south of the Altamaha River.

Most of this fell with the first round of precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak front was situated over north central Georgia early in the morning on the 11th.  A weak area of low pressure developed along the front during the day, triggering several showers and thunderstorms.  Lightning struck and injured a woman in Lumpkin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lumpkin County Emergency Manager reported that a woman was struck by lightning while trimming hedges at a location off Crawford Falls Road off GA Highway 9.  The woman was transported to the hospital and died as a result of her injuries on July 15, 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of short waves in a strong northwesterly upper-level flow pattern, along with moderate to high instability, combined to produce isolated to scattered reports of thunderstorm wind damage across North and Central Georgia each afternoon and evening. Localized flash flooding was reported in Fannin County of North Central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walton County 911 Center reported couple of trees down along H D Atha Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of short waves in a strong northwesterly upper-level flow pattern, along with moderate to high instability, combined to produce isolated to scattered reports of thunderstorm wind damage across North and Central Georgia each afternoon and evening. Localized flash flooding was reported in Fannin County of North Central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported several trees down along Hickory Nut Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the afternoon and evening hours along the Savannah River and moved southeastward across southeast Georgia. The storms developed in the region between the departing Hurricane Arthur to the northeast and a cold front approaching from the west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees reported down around Johnny Mercer Road on Wilmington Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of short waves in a strong northwesterly upper-level flow pattern, along with moderate to high instability, combined to produce isolated to scattered reports of thunderstorm wind damage across North and Central Georgia each afternoon and evening. Localized flash flooding was reported in Fannin County of North Central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upson County 911 Center reported 1 tree down on Nelson Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability and strong shear associated with a deepening upper-level trough and surface low produced scattered thunderstorms across portions of Central Georgia. Several severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and several tornadoes occurred in the afternoon and early evening hours along and behind a warm front lifting north across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crawford County 911 Center reported numerous trees and power lines down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and surface cold front moved through the region, bringing a round of severe weather across the area.  Isolated supercells developed ahead of the main squall line, and one produced an EF2 tornado in Florida which was the longest continuous damage path in Florida since the Central Florida outbreak of February 1, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down on Highway 93 near the intersection with Highway 37.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability and strong shear associated with a deepening upper-level trough and surface low produced scattered thunderstorms across portions of Central Georgia. Several severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and several tornadoes occurred in the afternoon and early evening hours along and behind a warm front lifting north across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bibb County 911 Center reported trees and power lines down on Zebulon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed across the Southeast on the 20th and 21st, with a QLCS (quasi-linear convective system) moving into northwest Georgia just after midnight on the 21st.  Scattered reports of wind damage were received across north Georgia through the early morning hours.  By mid-morning, the QLCS had pushed into central Georgia and intensified as instability increased, with a high-end EF2 tornado touching down in Laurens and Johnson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dade County 911 Center reported that several trees were blown down along Highway 11 South.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed west of the area before shifting into a warm and moist environment well ahead of a cold front sweeping through the southeastern United States.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Liberty County 911 Call Center reported a tree down along Live Oak Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample moisture and strong afternoon heating produced moderate to strong instability each afternoon with scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across North and Central Georgia. Slow moving thunderstorms with heavy rain setup over portions of North Georgia early in the morning on September 3rd. Rainfall amounts from 4 to 6 inches occurred in the Dalton area and caused flash flooding. Another slow moving thunderstorm with heavy rain produced isolated flash flooding south of Calhoun in the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whitfield County Deputy Emergency Manager reported numerous reports of flooded roads and small drainage creeks in the Dalton area. Heavy rain of 4 to 6 inches caused flood waters to enter some buildings including a church and a car dealership on North Glenwood Avenue. A roof collapsed at a carpet plant on 5th Avenue due to the rain which flooded the building. Firefighters rescued several motorists stranded in high water on Willowdale Road. A USGS stream gage on Mill Creek at Chattanooga Avenue on the north side of Dalton reached flood stage of 11 feet.  It crest at 11.7 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture persisted across the state. Weak upper-level waves along with strong afternoon heating combined to produce isolated strong to severe thunderstorms each afternoon and evening. Some of these slow moving thunderstorms produce heavy rain of 3 to 5 inches which caused isolated flash flooding in Atlanta metro counties and in Columbus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stage height of a USGS stream gage east of Atlanta on the North Fork Peachtree Creek at Buford Highway reached flood stage of 13 feet. The creek crest at 15.2 feet. Moderate flooding occurred along the creek. Large portions of the backyards of residential homes on Victory Drive...Dunwoody Place and Converse Drive flooded with around two feet of water. A portion of the Sun Tan Center parking lot near the gage flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant, deep moisture and a series of short waves moving through the region in a generally zonal mid and upper-level flow pattern produced isolated to scattered reports of thunderstorm wind damage across North and Central Georgia each day, mainly during the afternoon and evening hours when instability was maximized due to diurnal heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported numerous trees down across Fulton County. Locations included Bluefield Road at Wyland Drive, Donald Lee Hollowell Pkwy, West Wesley Road at Northside Drive, Paces Ferry Road, Mount Gilead Road at Campbellton Road, and Lucile Avenue at Lawton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant, deep moisture and a series of short waves moving through the region in a generally zonal mid and upper-level flow pattern produced isolated to scattered reports of thunderstorm wind damage across North and Central Georgia each day, mainly during the afternoon and evening hours when instability was maximized due to diurnal heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Center reported numerous trees and power lines down across the county, including along Richard Road, Shallowford Road, Lagoon Way and Dunleith Court.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant, deep moisture and a series of short waves moving through the region in a generally zonal mid and upper-level flow pattern produced isolated to scattered reports of thunderstorm wind damage across North and Central Georgia each day, mainly during the afternoon and evening hours when instability was maximized due to diurnal heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Greene County 911 Center reported many trees down and vehicle accidents countywide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread areas of precipitation with embedded thunderstorms developed in advance of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mobile home on Ben Grady Collins Road had windows blown out due to a thunderstorm wind gust.  Skirting was also ripped off the mobile home and a tricycle was blown into a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous spring system pushed across the Southeast on the 14th and 15th, with a cold front extending from the Ohio Valley into the southeast states.  Moderate instability combined with moderate shallow and deep-layer shear allowed of widespread showers and thunderstorms, several of which became severe with several reports of wind damage.  Two tornadoes occurred as well.  Training thunderstorms occurred over Bleckley County the evening of the 14th with intense rainfall of 4 to 6 inches. Isolated flash flooding caused some minor damage to two homes in the Cochran area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey team determined that an EF0 anticyclonic tornado touched down near the Dodge County Hospital off Plaza Avenue, initially uprooting trees and peeling back the roof of a nursing home.  The tornado continue on a northward track, causing minor roof, siding, and gutter damage to several community homes and downing large tree branches just east of Georgia Highway 117.  A front porch of a home off Harrell Avenue collapsed as a result of the winds.  Several trees were downed or uprooted, and a brick building and CVS store near the intersection of Highway 117 and Highway 23/Oak Street sustained roof loss.  The tornado continued northward causing roof and gutter damage to numerous residential and commercial buildings in Eastman, east of Oak Street and 5th Avenue/Georgia Highway 46.  Additional tree damage was observed north of Eastman-Dublin Highway near 7th Avenue.  After downing trees near a home at the intersection of Shady Acres and Old Eastman-Dublin Road, the tornado lift.  Maximum winds were estimated at 80 MPH.|[05/14/14: Tornado #1, County #1-1, EF0, Dodge, 2014:006].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to occasionally numerous showers and thunderstorms developed in the afternoon hours across southeast Georgia in the vicinity of a lingering cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported hail up to the size of quarters that last for 7 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during early night hours as a collision of seabreezes occurred from the Georgia coast and northern Florida coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tattnall County Dispatch relayed several reports of trees and power lines down on Old Reidsville-Collins Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of short waves in a strong northwesterly upper-level flow pattern, along with moderate to high instability, combined to produce isolated to scattered reports of thunderstorm wind damage across North and Central Georgia each afternoon and evening. Localized flash flooding was reported in Fannin County of North Central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported Penny-sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm in southeast Burke county produced quarter size hail and wind damage taking down trees and powerlines from Alexandria and Sardis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Georgia Power reported powerlines down in the town of Sardis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of shortwaves moved across the area on the 6th.  Plentiful moisture was in place and with a weak surface boundary sliding south across the area, another round of widespread showers and thunderstorms was able to develop.  Several of these became severe with damaging wind gusts reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Butts County Emergency Manager reported that two trees were blown down in the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: May 31st was another day of typical summertime convection, with the focus more across eastern Georgia due to the remnants of a weak backdoor front across the area. Several showers and thunderstorms were able to develop, and a couple of these became severe with damaging winds reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Newton County 911 Center reported two trees and two power lines down in the southwest part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon convection developed across the area with a few severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down on Martin Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong long wave trough was centered over the mid Mississippi Valley extending south to the Gulf coast with several shortwaves rotating around it. This led to occasional severe weather across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down across Lee county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The typical scattered afternoon convection in summertime resulted in a few severe storms with trees and power lines blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near Thomasville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed across the Southeast on the 20th and 21st, with a QLCS (quasi-linear convective system) moving into northwest Georgia just after midnight on the 21st.  Scattered reports of wind damage were received across north Georgia through the early morning hours.  By mid-morning, the QLCS had pushed into central Georgia and intensified as instability increased, with a high-end EF2 tornado touching down in Laurens and Johnson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media and the public reported multiple trees down in the Marietta area, including West Paces Ferry Road, Windy Hill Road, and Lower Roswell Road.  At the intersection of Oak Knoll Drive and Cross Gate Drive, multiple trees were down, including one that fell on a home and injured a child.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper ridge continued in place across the Southeast, with a weak shortwave pushing down the northwest flow aloft.  Once again, scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed and a few of these became severe with downed trees reported.  Additionally, thunderstorms produced around two inches of rain in less than two hours in south central Gilmer County.  The heavy rain fell over wet ground from previous rain the day before.  Rapid runoff caused isolated flash flooding in that area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Twiggs County 911 Center reported trees down on Highway 18 at mile marker 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed along an upstream outflow boundary early, then swept through a high-shear environment in southeast Georgia late morning into early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Chatham County Emergency Manager reported a tree down on a power line near the intersection of Godsells Street and Mildred Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving through the southeast U.S. and the Tennessee Valley forced abundant gulf moisture into Georgia early Christmas Eve Day. Widespread rain with embedded thunderstorms trained over Crisp County producing 3 to 4 inches of rain in less than ten hours. Isolated flash flooding occurred in the Cordele area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COCORAHS observer reported a 24-hour rainfall total of 3.10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of shortwaves produced widespread showers and thunderstorms on the 10th, several of which became severe with mainly damaging winds reported.  One severe thunderstorm occurred during the overnight hours, and several more fired during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Butts County Emergency Manager reported numerous trees down in Jackson.  Two or three trees fell on a house and a large limb fell on a fire station awning.
